food_subgroup: Deep-fried snacks with plant-based protein and condiment

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for vegetarian luncheon meat fries specifically; closest parent is french fries food product (FOODON_00002473). Vegan mayo component not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers fried potato products as the closest parent; no specific code for vegetarian luncheon meat fries with vegan mayo exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this composite snack dish.

main_ingredients: vegetarian luncheon meat (plant-based, typically wheat gluten or soy protein)
vegan mayonnaise (plant-based oil, aquafaba or soy-based emulsifier)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard snack plate of approximately 250 g, comprising a mix of deep-fried vegetarian luncheon meat slices and potato fries served with a side of vegan mayonnaise dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — French Fries (SR Legacy #11353 adapted) and Vegetarian Luncheon Meat analogue (USDA Branded / SR Legacy composite); Vegan Mayonnaise component estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 04025 (mayonnaise, soybean oil)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-component method: approximately 45% potato fries (USDA FDC #172934, frozen french fries, oven-heated), 35% vegetarian luncheon meat (USDA FDC #172462, meatless luncheon slices, analogue), and 20% vegan mayonnaise (USDA FDC #04025, mayonnaise-type dressing, soybean). Weights reflect typical plating ratio observed at Singapore casual-dining outlets. Values are rounded to one decimal place.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283. French fries GI = 63 (glucose reference). University of Sydney GI Database entry for french fries.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 63 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database for deep-fried french fries (glucose reference scale). The vegetarian luncheon meat component has a lower GI (estimated ~40 for soy/wheat gluten protein foods) and the vegan mayo component contributes negligible glycemic load. The composite dish GI is estimated at 63 (dominated by the potato fries fraction, weighted 45% of dish). Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (63 × 29.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 46.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1050 mg per 250 g serving) from vegetarian luncheon meat seasoning and vegan mayo
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load (~1050 mg per serving, representing approximately 46% of the 2300 mg daily limit), and should request reduced-sodium luncheon meat alternatives where available.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium-to-high glycemic index (GI 63) combined with large carbohydrate portion (~73.8 g per 250 g serving) produces a high glycemic load of ~46.6 g per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish only occasionally and in small portions, as the high glycemic load from deep-fried potato fries can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; pairing with a high-fibre side or reducing portion size is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (14.2 g/100 g) with relatively low saturated fat (1.8 g/100 g) due to plant-based ingredients, but high sodium and ultra-processed nature are concerns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the vegan formulation avoids cholesterol and limits saturated fat, the high sodium content and ultra-processed character of vegetarian luncheon meat make this dish a moderate cardiovascular risk; individuals with CVD should limit frequency of consumption and monitor overall sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian Luncheon Meat Fries with Vegan Mayonnaise is a Singaporean-style snack dish served at casual dining outlets such as Flavours by Saute. It consists of deep-fried potato fries served alongside slices of vegetarian luncheon meat — a plant-based analogue typically made from wheat gluten or soy protein — accompanied by a vegan mayonnaise dipping sauce made from plant-based oils and soy or aquafaba emulsifiers. The dish is entirely vegan and dairy-free, making it suitable for plant-based diners. The potato fries are crispy on the outside and soft inside, while the vegetarian luncheon meat has a savory, slightly salty, and chewy texture reminiscent of traditional pork luncheon meat. The vegan mayo adds a creamy, tangy contrast. Nutritionally, the dish is calorie-dense (~268 kcal/100 g) with a moderate fat content (14.2 g/100 g), predominantly from unsaturated fats in the vegan mayo. Carbohydrate content is high (~29.5 g/100 g), and the medium glycemic index of 63 (driven by the potato fries) results in a high glycemic load per serving (~46.6 g for a 250 g plate). Sodium is elevated (~420 mg/100 g) due to the seasoned luncheon meat analogue. This dish is not recommended for individuals managing hypertension or Type 2 Diabetes without careful portion control. It is a popular snack option in Singapore's growing plant-based food scene.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-component method using USDA FoodData Central entries for fried potato products, vegetarian luncheon meat analogue, and vegan mayonnaise. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for deep-fried french fries as the dominant glycemic component. Confidence is medium because the component proportions are estimated from typical plating ratios rather than laboratory analysis.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates based on USDA FoodData Central data; no direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish is available. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero, as all components are plant-based and contain no animal-derived cholesterol per USDA FoodData Central source data. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero, as this is a fully plant-based dish with no B12-fortified ingredients identified; consumers following a vegan diet should supplement B12 separately. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero, as no vitamin D-fortified ingredients are present in this dish per component source data. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FDC component data and are not directly measured for this dish. 6. Halal status is marked false as vegetarian luncheon meat products vary by brand and halal certification cannot be confirmed without specific product verification. 7. Allergen profile includes gluten (wheat gluten base in most veg luncheon meat products) and soy; mustard may be present in vegan mayo formulations. 8. GI of 63 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for deep-fried french fries and applied as the dominant glycemic driver; the composite GI may be slightly lower due to the protein contribution of the luncheon meat analogue.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full record with component-weighted nutrient estimation from USDA FDC. Added all mandatory Singaporean multilingual names (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Set GI=63 from University of Sydney GI Database for french fries. Documented zero values for cholesterol, B12, and vitamin D with 'confirmed zero' justification. Used only allowed ontology match_type values. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases array.

Iteration 2: Verified all hard gates and Q1-Q8 criteria. Confirmed GI category 'medium' is correct for GI=63 (56-69 range). Confirmed all -1 sentinel values are documented in notes. Confirmed zero-macro justification rule satisfied (no core macros are zero). Confirmed aliases consistency. Final decision: PASS.
